title: Window Functions
description: Ranking, aggregate, and value window functions with OVER, PARTITION BY, and frame specifications

concepts.title: Ranking Functions

sqlServer.code:
-- Create sample sales data for demonstration
CREATE TABLE #SalesData (
    SalesRep    VARCHAR(50),
    Region      VARCHAR(50),
    SaleAmount  DECIMAL(10,2)
);

INSERT INTO #SalesData VALUES
    ('Alice',   'East',  9500.00),
    ('Bob',     'East',  8200.00),
    ('Carol',   'East',  8200.00),
    ('Dave',    'West',  11000.00),
    ('Eve',     'West',  9800.00),
    ('Frank',   'West',  7600.00),
    ('Grace',   'West',  7600.00);

-- ROW_NUMBER: unique sequential rank (ties broken arbitrarily)
-- RANK: same rank for ties, gaps after ties
-- DENSE_RANK: same rank for ties, no gaps
-- NTILE(4): divide rows into 4 quartiles
SELECT
    SalesRep,
    Region,
    SaleAmount,
    ROW_NUMBER()  OVER (PARTITION BY Region ORDER BY SaleAmount DESC) AS RowNum,
    RANK()        OVER (PARTITION BY Region ORDER BY SaleAmount DESC) AS RankNum,
    DENSE_RANK()  OVER (PARTITION BY Region ORDER BY SaleAmount DESC) AS DenseRankNum,
    NTILE(4)      OVER (ORDER BY SaleAmount DESC)                     AS Quartile
FROM #SalesData
ORDER BY Region, SaleAmount DESC;

-- Find duplicate rows using ROW_NUMBER
SELECT SalesRep, Region, SaleAmount
FROM (
    SELECT *,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY Region, SaleAmount ORDER BY SalesRep) AS rn
    FROM #SalesData
) t
WHERE rn > 1;

DROP TABLE #SalesData;
sqlServer.description: SQL Server supports ROW_NUMBER(), RANK(), DENSE_RANK(), and NTILE() window functions using the OVER clause with optional PARTITION BY and required ORDER BY. ROW_NUMBER() assigns a unique sequential integer to each row; RANK() assigns the same rank to ties with gaps afterward; DENSE_RANK() assigns the same rank to ties without gaps; NTILE(n) distributes rows into n roughly equal buckets. These functions are commonly used for pagination, deduplication, and percentile assignment.
notes: ROW_NUMBER() is ideal for pagination and deduplication — always unique within partition
RANK() leaves gaps after ties (1,1,3), DENSE_RANK() does not (1,1,2)
NTILE() splits the ordered set into n buckets; uneven rows go to lower-numbered buckets
PARTITION BY is optional — omitting it applies the ranking across the entire result set

postgres.code:
-- Create sample sales data for demonstration
CREATE TEMP TABLE sales_data (
    sales_rep   VARCHAR(50),
    region      VARCHAR(50),
    sale_amount NUMERIC(10,2)
);

INSERT INTO sales_data VALUES
    ('Alice',   'East',  9500.00),
    ('Bob',     'East',  8200.00),
    ('Carol',   'East',  8200.00),
    ('Dave',    'West',  11000.00),
    ('Eve',     'West',  9800.00),
    ('Frank',   'West',  7600.00),
    ('Grace',   'West',  7600.00);

-- ROW_NUMBER, RANK, DENSE_RANK, NTILE: identical syntax to SQL Server
SELECT
    sales_rep,
    region,
    sale_amount,
    ROW_NUMBER()  OVER (PARTITION BY region ORDER BY sale_amount DESC) AS row_num,
    RANK()        OVER (PARTITION BY region ORDER BY sale_amount DESC) AS rank_num,
    DENSE_RANK()  OVER (PARTITION BY region ORDER BY sale_amount DESC) AS dense_rank_num,
    NTILE(4)      OVER (ORDER BY sale_amount DESC)                     AS quartile
FROM sales_data
ORDER BY region, sale_amount DESC;

-- Find duplicate rows using ROW_NUMBER
SELECT sales_rep, region, sale_amount
FROM (
    SELECT *,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY region, sale_amount ORDER BY sales_rep) AS rn
    FROM sales_data
) t
WHERE rn > 1;
postgres.description: PostgreSQL supports the same ROW_NUMBER(), RANK(), DENSE_RANK(), and NTILE() ranking functions with identical OVER (PARTITION BY ... ORDER BY ...) syntax. The behavior is the same: ROW_NUMBER() is always unique, RANK() has gaps after ties, DENSE_RANK() has no gaps, and NTILE(n) divides rows into n buckets. PostgreSQL uses snake_case conventions for column aliases by convention but the function names are identical.
keyDifferences: Syntax is identical between SQL Server and PostgreSQL for all four ranking functions
PostgreSQL uses TEMP TABLE instead of # prefix for temporary tables
Column naming convention in PostgreSQL favors snake_case (row_num vs RowNum)
notes: All four ranking functions (ROW_NUMBER, RANK, DENSE_RANK, NTILE) have identical syntax in both platforms
PostgreSQL TEMP TABLE is scoped to the session, equivalent to SQL Server # temp tables
The deduplication pattern using ROW_NUMBER in a subquery works identically in both platforms

concepts.keyDifference: Ranking function syntax (ROW_NUMBER, RANK, DENSE_RANK, NTILE) is identical between SQL Server and PostgreSQL — only temporary table syntax differs (# prefix vs TEMP TABLE keyword).

concepts.title: Aggregate Window Functions

sqlServer.code:
-- Sample monthly sales data
CREATE TABLE #MonthlySales (
    SaleMonth   DATE,
    Region      VARCHAR(50),
    Revenue     DECIMAL(12,2)
);

INSERT INTO #MonthlySales VALUES
    ('2024-01-01', 'East', 42000.00),
    ('2024-02-01', 'East', 38500.00),
    ('2024-03-01', 'East', 51000.00),
    ('2024-01-01', 'West', 63000.00),
    ('2024-02-01', 'West', 59000.00),
    ('2024-03-01', 'West', 71000.00);

SELECT
    SaleMonth,
    Region,
    Revenue,
    -- Running total within region (cumulative sum)
    SUM(Revenue) OVER (
        PARTITION BY Region
        ORDER BY SaleMonth
        ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
    ) AS RunningTotal,
    -- 2-month moving average within region
    AVG(Revenue) OVER (
        PARTITION BY Region
        ORDER BY SaleMonth
        ROWS BETWEEN 1 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
    ) AS MovingAvg2Mo,
    -- Count of rows seen so far within region
    COUNT(*) OVER (
        PARTITION BY Region
        ORDER BY SaleMonth
        ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
    ) AS CumulativeCount,
    -- Min and Max across the entire result (no partition)
    MIN(Revenue) OVER () AS GlobalMin,
    MAX(Revenue) OVER () AS GlobalMax
FROM #MonthlySales
ORDER BY Region, SaleMonth;

DROP TABLE #MonthlySales;
sqlServer.description: SQL Server supports aggregate functions (SUM, AVG, COUNT, MIN, MAX) as window functions using the OVER clause. Adding ORDER BY inside OVER creates a running/cumulative aggregate; the ROWS BETWEEN frame clause controls which rows participate. ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW is the most common frame for running totals. Omitting ORDER BY and ROWS gives a grand aggregate of the entire partition.
notes: Without ORDER BY in OVER(), the aggregate applies to the entire partition — useful for percent-of-total calculations
ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW is the default when ORDER BY is present in SQL Server
Use ROWS (physical row count) rather than RANGE (logical value range) for predictable moving averages
Window functions are computed after WHERE and GROUP BY but before ORDER BY

postgres.code:
-- Sample monthly sales data
CREATE TEMP TABLE monthly_sales (
    sale_month  DATE,
    region      VARCHAR(50),
    revenue     NUMERIC(12,2)
);

INSERT INTO monthly_sales VALUES
    ('2024-01-01', 'East', 42000.00),
    ('2024-02-01', 'East', 38500.00),
    ('2024-03-01', 'East', 51000.00),
    ('2024-01-01', 'West', 63000.00),
    ('2024-02-01', 'West', 59000.00),
    ('2024-03-01', 'West', 71000.00);

SELECT
    sale_month,
    region,
    revenue,
    -- Running total within region
    SUM(revenue) OVER (
        PARTITION BY region
        ORDER BY sale_month
        ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
    ) AS running_total,
    -- 2-month moving average within region
    AVG(revenue) OVER (
        PARTITION BY region
        ORDER BY sale_month
        ROWS BETWEEN 1 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
    ) AS moving_avg_2mo,
    -- Count of rows seen so far within region
    COUNT(*) OVER (
        PARTITION BY region
        ORDER BY sale_month
        ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
    ) AS cumulative_count,
    -- Min and Max across entire result set
    MIN(revenue) OVER () AS global_min,
    MAX(revenue) OVER () AS global_max
FROM monthly_sales
ORDER BY region, sale_month;
postgres.description: PostgreSQL supports the same aggregate window functions (SUM, AVG, COUNT, MIN, MAX) with identical OVER clause syntax including PARTITION BY, ORDER BY, and ROWS BETWEEN frame specifications. The behavior and frame semantics are identical to SQL Server. PostgreSQL also supports a WINDOW clause to name and reuse window definitions, which can reduce repetition when using the same frame multiple times.
keyDifferences: Syntax is identical for all aggregate window functions and frame specifications
PostgreSQL supports a named WINDOW clause (WINDOW w AS (PARTITION BY region ORDER BY sale_month)) to avoid repeating OVER definitions
PostgreSQL uses NUMERIC instead of DECIMAL for exact numeric types (both are equivalent)
notes: All ROWS BETWEEN frame syntax is identical between SQL Server and PostgreSQL
PostgreSQL WINDOW clause: SELECT SUM(revenue) OVER w FROM t WINDOW w AS (PARTITION BY region ORDER BY sale_month)
The RANGE vs ROWS distinction works identically in both platforms — ROWS is generally preferred for predictability

concepts.keyDifference: Aggregate window function syntax (SUM, AVG, COUNT, MIN, MAX with OVER, PARTITION BY, ROWS BETWEEN) is identical in both platforms. PostgreSQL adds a convenient WINDOW clause for naming and reusing window frame definitions.

concepts.title: LAG and LEAD

sqlServer.code:
-- Monthly revenue data for period-over-period analysis
CREATE TABLE #Sales (
    SaleMonth   DATE,
    Revenue     DECIMAL(12,2)
);

INSERT INTO #Sales VALUES
    ('2024-01-01', 95000.00),
    ('2024-02-01', 87000.00),
    ('2024-03-01', 112000.00),
    ('2024-04-01', 98000.00),
    ('2024-05-01', 134000.00);

SELECT
    SaleMonth,
    Revenue,
    -- LAG(column, offset, default): access previous row value
    LAG(Revenue, 1, 0)  OVER (ORDER BY SaleMonth) AS PrevMonthRevenue,
    -- LEAD(column, offset, default): access next row value
    LEAD(Revenue, 1, 0) OVER (ORDER BY SaleMonth) AS NextMonthRevenue,
    -- Month-over-month change (use Revenue as default to get 0 change for first row)
    Revenue - LAG(Revenue, 1, Revenue) OVER (ORDER BY SaleMonth) AS MoMChange,
    -- Month-over-month percent change
    CAST(
        ROUND(
            100.0 * (Revenue - LAG(Revenue, 1, Revenue) OVER (ORDER BY SaleMonth))
            / LAG(Revenue, 1, Revenue) OVER (ORDER BY SaleMonth),
        2) AS DECIMAL(8,2)
    ) AS MoMPctChange
FROM #Sales
ORDER BY SaleMonth;

DROP TABLE #Sales;
sqlServer.description: LAG() accesses the value from a previous row within the partition; LEAD() accesses the value from a following row. Both accept three parameters: the column expression, the offset (number of rows back/forward, default 1), and a default value returned when the offset goes beyond the partition boundary. LAG and LEAD are essential for period-over-period comparisons without self-joins.
notes: LAG(col, 1, default) and LEAD(col, 1, default) — offset defaults to 1 if omitted
The default value parameter prevents NULL when the offset falls outside the partition boundary
Using the same column as the default (LAG(Revenue, 1, Revenue)) returns 0 change for the first row
LAG/LEAD do not require a frame specification — they always reference a specific offset row

postgres.code:
-- Monthly revenue data for period-over-period analysis
CREATE TEMP TABLE sales (
    sale_month  DATE,
    revenue     NUMERIC(12,2)
);

INSERT INTO sales VALUES
    ('2024-01-01', 95000.00),
    ('2024-02-01', 87000.00),
    ('2024-03-01', 112000.00),
    ('2024-04-01', 98000.00),
    ('2024-05-01', 134000.00);

SELECT
    sale_month,
    revenue,
    -- LAG(column, offset, default): access previous row value
    LAG(revenue, 1, 0::NUMERIC)  OVER (ORDER BY sale_month) AS prev_month_revenue,
    -- LEAD(column, offset, default): access next row value
    LEAD(revenue, 1, 0::NUMERIC) OVER (ORDER BY sale_month) AS next_month_revenue,
    -- Month-over-month change
    revenue - LAG(revenue, 1, revenue) OVER (ORDER BY sale_month) AS mom_change,
    -- Month-over-month percent change
    ROUND(
        100.0 * (revenue - LAG(revenue, 1, revenue) OVER (ORDER BY sale_month))
        / LAG(revenue, 1, revenue) OVER (ORDER BY sale_month),
    2) AS mom_pct_change
FROM sales
ORDER BY sale_month;
postgres.description: PostgreSQL LAG() and LEAD() are identical in syntax and behavior to SQL Server. Both accept the column expression, offset, and default value parameters. The only practical difference is that PostgreSQL requires explicit type casting for the default value when the literal type is ambiguous (e.g., 0::NUMERIC to match a NUMERIC column), whereas SQL Server performs implicit conversion.
keyDifferences: LAG() and LEAD() syntax and behavior are identical between SQL Server and PostgreSQL
PostgreSQL may require explicit casting for the default value parameter (e.g., 0::NUMERIC) to avoid type mismatch errors
PostgreSQL does not require CAST(...AS DECIMAL) wrappers — ROUND() returns NUMERIC directly
notes: The default value type must match the column type in PostgreSQL — use 0::NUMERIC or 0.0 for numeric columns
Both platforms support PARTITION BY inside LAG/LEAD OVER clauses for partitioned period comparisons
LAG with offset > 1 accesses rows further back: LAG(revenue, 3) compares to 3 months prior

concepts.keyDifference: LAG() and LEAD() syntax is identical in both platforms. PostgreSQL requires the default value to be explicitly typed (0::NUMERIC) while SQL Server handles the implicit conversion automatically.

concepts.title: FIRST_VALUE and LAST_VALUE

sqlServer.code:
-- Sales rep performance data
CREATE TABLE #RegionSales (
    SalesRep    VARCHAR(50),
    Region      VARCHAR(50),
    Revenue     DECIMAL(12,2)
);

INSERT INTO #RegionSales VALUES
    ('Alice', 'East', 42000.00),
    ('Bob',   'East', 38500.00),
    ('Carol', 'East', 51000.00),
    ('Dave',  'West', 63000.00),
    ('Eve',   'West', 59000.00),
    ('Frank', 'West', 71000.00);

SELECT
    SalesRep,
    Region,
    Revenue,
    -- FIRST_VALUE: top performer in region (default frame works correctly)
    FIRST_VALUE(SalesRep) OVER (
        PARTITION BY Region ORDER BY Revenue DESC
    ) AS TopRep,
    -- LAST_VALUE: bottom performer — requires explicit UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING frame!
    -- Without this, the default frame (RANGE UNBOUNDED PRECEDING TO CURRENT ROW)
    -- makes LAST_VALUE return the current row, not the last row in the partition.
    LAST_VALUE(SalesRep) OVER (
        PARTITION BY Region ORDER BY Revenue DESC
        ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING
    ) AS BottomRep,
    FIRST_VALUE(Revenue) OVER (
        PARTITION BY Region ORDER BY Revenue DESC
    ) AS MaxRevenue,
    LAST_VALUE(Revenue) OVER (
        PARTITION BY Region ORDER BY Revenue DESC
        ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING
    ) AS MinRevenue
FROM #RegionSales
ORDER BY Region, Revenue DESC;

DROP TABLE #RegionSales;
sqlServer.description: FIRST_VALUE() returns the value from the first row of the window frame; LAST_VALUE() returns the value from the last row. FIRST_VALUE() works as expected with the default frame because the first row is always in scope. LAST_VALUE() is the common gotcha: the default frame is RANGE BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW, so it returns the current row's value, not the partition's last row. Always specify ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING with LAST_VALUE().
notes: FIRST_VALUE() works correctly with the default frame — the first row is always included
LAST_VALUE() with default frame returns the CURRENT ROW value, not the partition's last row — a very common bug
Always pair LAST_VALUE() with ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING
An alternative to LAST_VALUE() is FIRST_VALUE() with the ORDER BY reversed

postgres.code:
-- Sales rep performance data
CREATE TEMP TABLE region_sales (
    sales_rep   VARCHAR(50),
    region      VARCHAR(50),
    revenue     NUMERIC(12,2)
);

INSERT INTO region_sales VALUES
    ('Alice', 'East', 42000.00),
    ('Bob',   'East', 38500.00),
    ('Carol', 'East', 51000.00),
    ('Dave',  'West', 63000.00),
    ('Eve',   'West', 59000.00),
    ('Frank', 'West', 71000.00);

SELECT
    sales_rep,
    region,
    revenue,
    -- FIRST_VALUE: top performer (default frame is sufficient)
    FIRST_VALUE(sales_rep) OVER (
        PARTITION BY region ORDER BY revenue DESC
    ) AS top_rep,
    -- LAST_VALUE: REQUIRES explicit frame in PostgreSQL too!
    -- Same gotcha as SQL Server: default frame stops at current row
    LAST_VALUE(sales_rep) OVER (
        PARTITION BY region ORDER BY revenue DESC
        ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING
    ) AS bottom_rep,
    FIRST_VALUE(revenue) OVER (
        PARTITION BY region ORDER BY revenue DESC
    ) AS max_revenue,
    LAST_VALUE(revenue) OVER (
        PARTITION BY region ORDER BY revenue DESC
        ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING
    ) AS min_revenue
FROM region_sales
ORDER BY region, revenue DESC;
postgres.description: PostgreSQL FIRST_VALUE() and LAST_VALUE() have identical syntax and the same frame behavior as SQL Server. The LAST_VALUE() gotcha is the same in both platforms: without an explicit ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING frame, LAST_VALUE() returns the current row's value rather than the last row in the partition. This is one of the most common window function bugs in both SQL Server and PostgreSQL.
keyDifferences: FIRST_VALUE() and LAST_VALUE() syntax is identical between SQL Server and PostgreSQL
The LAST_VALUE() default frame bug affects both platforms equally — always specify ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING
An idiomatic PostgreSQL alternative: use FIRST_VALUE() with DESC/ASC reversed instead of LAST_VALUE()
notes: The frame default issue with LAST_VALUE() is a cross-platform gotcha — it affects both SQL Server and PostgreSQL
Safer alternative: FIRST_VALUE(revenue) OVER (PARTITION BY region ORDER BY revenue ASC) gives the minimum without the frame issue
PostgreSQL WINDOW clause can define the frame once and reuse it across multiple FIRST_VALUE/LAST_VALUE calls

concepts.keyDifference: FIRST_VALUE() and LAST_VALUE() syntax is identical in both platforms. The critical gotcha — LAST_VALUE() returning the current row instead of the partition's last row when using the default frame — exists in both SQL Server and PostgreSQL. Always specify ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING with LAST_VALUE().
